course_description:
This is an issues-based course where students apply their developing sociology skills to real world problems and injustices such as gender discrimination, racism, and income inequality, in Australia and globally. The course content will be dynamic, using current debates and issues as the basis for learning. The assessment will be tailored to address the interests of students. Learning will take place in a blended learning environment with a mixture of lectures, online material, and in-class workshop engagement. The course utilises a collaborative environment that invites students to apply their sociological imaginations to think about how power, social structure, and identity work to create and to resist social inequalities.

Acknowledgements:

I would like to acknowledge the traditional owners of the lands on which this university now stands. We give our respect to the elders of all indigenous people including those who live in this city, now separated from their lands through a colonial history of state-sponsored and interpersonal ignorance and violence.

course_contacts.name: Associate Professor Lynda Shevellar
course_contacts.notes: Your tutor will advise their consultation availability. Please note that I do not have set drop-in hours, but prefer to negotiate a mutually convenient consultation time to work with both our timetables. You can set up a solo time or meet me as a group if you would prefer. To set up a time please send me an email with "SOCY1070 Consultation" in the subject line. If the matter is urgent you will find the Blackboard Discussion Board an excellent way to receive fast responses to the majority of your queries. Not only will I - or your tutor - attend to your concerns quickly, but other students will also have an opportunity to respond, to clarify issues of concern and to learn from your inquiry.

course_contacts.name: Student Enquiries School of Social Science
course_contacts.notes: Level 3, Michie Building (09), St Lucia campus, The University of Queensland. Monday-Friday, 9:00am-12:00pm, 1:00pm-4:00pm.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
Please note that the weekly workshops for this course commence in Week 1. Consult the official UQ timetable for class dates and times. Classes will start promptly to make the most of our limited time together. Please make sure you locate the building and class before the class to ensure you do not miss important information.
If you are unable to attend a classﾠplease check Blackboard and liaise with other students to obtain the relevant information. Due to the class size and other teaching responsibilities, I am unable to respond to individual requests for notes or provide a personal repeat of the weekly content. Interaction, discussion, and reflection are crucial to learning. It is not possible to reproduce the experience of the group.ﾠNotes and slides in this course are provided to support and complement your learning however they are not intended as a substitute for experience.
NOTE: It is your responsibility to activate and check your UQ email account regularly. Important messages concerning course materials, lectures, seminars, assessments, or library resources may be communicated via email. Please put ‘SOCY1070’ in the subject line of any email correspondence with the teaching staff. If you do not do this, the email may not be answered promptly or at all.
Teaching staff do not have access to the timetabling system to help with class allocation. Therefore, should you need help with your timetable and/or allocation of classes, please ensure you email the School of Social Science Administration Team at
student.socsci@uq.edu.au
from your UQ student email account with the following details: full name, student ID, and course code.
course_aims: In this issues-based course, students will apply their developing sociology skills to real world problems of inequality, such as gender discrimination, racism, and rising income inequality, to explore the possibilities for change. Completion of this course will allow students to progress to second year where we expect they will be able to approach more applied methods and sub-discipline based electives with more confidence and better outcomes.

learning_outcomes.description: demonstrate an ability to think sociologically about inequality, including what social inequality is, why it occurs, what its impact is, and possible responses
critically reflect on your own experiences and positionality in relation to inequality and privilege
develop a sense of agency and engage with solution focused social science
develop communication skills to advocate for social justice initiatives

assessment_summary.title: In-class writing task series
assessment_summary.due_date: Task 1 - ungraded (Week 2: during your workshop); Task 2 - graded (Week 5: during your workshop); Task 3 - graded (Week 8: during your workshop); Task 4 - graded (Week 11: during your workshop); The writing task is held during the student's scheduled workshop. Workshops occur on different days and at different times for different students.

assessment_summary.title: Video Pitch
assessment_summary.due_date: 31/10/2025 2:00 pm; As you will see in the Blackboard information about the task, this is an identity verified task, meaning you are required to be visible as the speaker in the video, and to clearly identify yourself at the start of the video.

assessment_details.title: In-class writing task series
assessment_details.due_date: Task 1 - ungraded (Week 2: during your workshop) Task 2 - graded (Week 5: during your workshop) Task 3 - graded (Week 8: during your workshop) Task 4 - graded (Week 11: during your workshop) The writing task is held during the student's scheduled workshop. Workshops occur on different days and at different times for different students.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 1 is a series of writing tasks that you will engage in during class time 4 times throughout the semester: * The first task, due in Week 2 of semester, is a practice task, so that you will know what to expect for the rest of semester. It is not graded. * The remaining 3 writing tasks are graded and are worth 20% each, making a total of 60% of your mark. The graded components will be due in class in Weeks 5, 8, and 11. The writing exercises will occur in the second half of the workshop. You will be given a maximum of 45 minutes to read through a short case study and respond to a question. Your response will be a maximum of 500 words. The writing task will be based on the work you have done in the modules and the in-class exercises and discussions that have occurred prior to the allocated workshop. This assessment will be completed in-class via Inspera. Students will be required to bring their own laptop computer, or borrow a laptop computer from the library Please see Blackboard for further details about the task and how it will be graded. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI): This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Please see the Study Guide for submission instructions. Note: we will be practicing the submission process during Week 2 workshops. If students experience unavoidable technical difficulties during the assessment task that prevent them from completing the assessment; alternative arrangements will be provided.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
This is an identity verified assessment activity and requires you to be in class to undertake and submit the assessment task. If you cannot be present during your usual workshop time, please see the Study Guide on Blackboard for details of alternative options for completing the task and talk to your tutor or course coordinator.
If you need additional time to complete writing tasks, please ensure you have an SAP through UQ Student Services and talk directly to the course coordinator to enable us to support you.
Please note that from Semester 2, 2025 the Assessment Procedure has changed. You must submit a request for an extension as soon as it becomes clear you need an extension. Your request should be submitted no later than the assessment item's due date and time.
The request must be accompanied by supporting documentation corroborating the reason for the request. A list of acceptable reasons for an extension and the evidence you must provide can be found here . Your request may be refused if you do not meet the acceptable reasons for an extension. The student submitting the request is fully responsible for all supporting documentation that is provided with the request and should ensure all documents are authentic.
Students who are registered with Student Support and Wellbeing Services may apply for an extension without providing documentation. This extension request must be the student’s first extension request for the assessment item. If you proceed with an extension request based on your SAP, you will be ineligible to use your discretionary extension for the same assessment item. In the School of Social Science, extensions on the basis of an approved Student Access Plan (SAP) can be approved for a maximum period of 7 calendar days. Subsequent extensions for a piece of assessment will require students to provide their SAP along with additional supporting documentation (e.g., a medical certificate or other supporting evidence listed on my.UQ ).
A student is eligible for a discretionary extension for one assessment task per semester for a duration of 2 calendar days or less. A discretionary extension may only be used on a student’s first extension request for an assessment task.
A student may have a maximum of 3 extension requests approved for a single assessment task. If a third extension is necessary, you must submit an Assessment Management Plan in addition to your supporting documentation with your request. In exceptional circumstances, a fourth extension may be requested through the grievance and appeals process.
Extension requests exceeding the maximum extension period stated for a piece of assessment will only be considered under exceptional circumstances (circumstances outside of your control) with additional supporting documentation.
Late applications must include evidence of the reasons for the late request, detailing why you were unable to apply for an extension by the due date and time. The School of Social Science will not accept personal statements.
Extension requests are processed and managed by the School of Social Science Administration Team.
Extensions in your final semester of study could delay your graduation by up to one semester.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .

assessment_details.title: Video Pitch
assessment_details.due_date: 31/10/2025 2:00 pm As you will see in the Blackboard information about the task, this is an identity verified task, meaning you are required to be visible as the speaker in the video, and to clearly identify yourself at the start of the video.
assessment_details.task_description: In this assignment, you will create a 3-minute video pitch in response to a scenario, to articulate the importance of responding to a particular area of social inequality. Drawing on concepts learned in the course, and your own research, you will identify a compelling argument for addressing systemic disparities and injustices in a particular area, propose a path of action and present your pitch in a concise and persuasive manner. Please see Blackboard for details of the task, including the scenario, submission requirements, and uploading instructions, as well as the marking criteria that will be used to assess your work. Note: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub . Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Introduction: Welcome & What is Social Inequality? Welcome to week 1 of SOCY1070 which serves as an introduction to the course. There is a learning module for Week 1 to help you get oriented to the course and to provide you with links to some helpful resources and support. You are expected to come to class having read the Course Profile and the Course Study Guide. Building on what you have learned from the Study Guide for the course, in this week’s workshop we look at how learning will happen in the course, what you need to do to pass the course, and the resources that will assist you. You will be invited to reflect on your personal connections to the topic of inequality through a critical incident timeline . We will then introduce two key theories that underpin this course.

Why does social inequality matter? Welcome to Week 2 of SOCY1070 where we look more closely at why social inequality matters, and why, as social scientists we might be interested in it. Our online modules introduce this topic with interviews, videos and readings. In the workshop we build on these to deepen discussion and think about the role our own values play in how we approach social inequality. In the second part of our workshop, we engage in Assessment 1 for the course. This is the first of four such in-class exercises. This week’s work is an opportunity to practice the assessment style, without the pressure of marks - as it will not be graded. It will give you an opportunity to understand how assessment will work and to ensure you can submit your work successfully in the weeks to come. You can see further details about Assessment 1 in the course Study Guide.

How do we know if/where social inequality exists? Measurement is one means by which we can recognise the existence of inequality, and understand whether it is changing – for better or worse. However, this is not a neutral, straightforward process. In this week’s learning module, Associate Professor “Paco” Perales takes us through the terrain of measuring inequality, via a series of video lectures. Please note that as Monday of this week is a public holiday in Queensland, there are no face-to-face workshops this week, only the online module for you to complete. Please reach out to your tutors should you need clarity about any of the module material.

What causes social inequality? Our focus this week is on the causes of social inequality. Through the online learning module, we begin with the idea of othering, and essentialising the other. We also distinguish between social categorisation and social devaluation and look at how judgement cycles are formed at the individual and social levels and explore the important topic of “unconscious bias”. Our workshop engages with the often uncomfortable topic of “othering” and the process of social devaluation. Please come to class having engaged with the online module, and a somewhat unusual reading, to prepare you for our case study on modern slavery.

Is social inequality inevitable? In this week’s online learning module we ask, “Is social inequality inevitable?” by thinking about the history of inequality and how it has manifested over time. We will also ask the question, “So, are things getting better or worse?” and consider whether we are getting closer to equality for all. You might be surprised at the answer. In the first half of today’s workshop we invite you to apply your learning from the module to begin Assessment 2. Assessment 2 is due in the final week of semester but will require ongoing work throughout the semester and our aim is to help you get started early. In the second half of the workshop we will undertake Assessment item 1(b). Assessment 1 is a series of 4 in-class writing exercises and today’s exercise will be the first graded component, worth 20% of your final mark. If you cannot make your workshop time, please see the Study Guide for options for how you can still complete this assessment item. Assessment 1(b) will focus on material from any of the previous weeks (Weeks 2-4).

Inequality and the impact of colonisation Last week we took a broad historical lens to the idea of social inequality. This week we narrow our focus to the specific topic of colonialisation and the impact of colonisation on First nations people. We do this by engaging with the excellent online resources “History lives in us”. This work examines how events and policies in the past have shaped the identities and experiences of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples today. The Module looks at Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander activism; the ‘rights’ movement and campaigns waged by key organisations in the lead up to the establishment of the Commonwealth Department of Aboriginal Affairs in 1972. This week’s workshop invites further engagement with the module and examines in what ways this history is still powerfully with us and the consequences for first nations people globally and in Australia.

Theories of inequality - Intersectionality and Privilege Often, sociology courses and texts on inequality provide a weekly run-down on different axes of inequality: gender in Week 1, race in Week 2, class in Week 3, and so on. In this course, we have deliberately chosen not to do that, but to instead foreground intersectional understandings of inequality. This week's learning module focuses on this idea by introducing the topics of positionality, privilege, intersectionality and compound disadvantage. We will continue this discussion in our workshop where we will invite you to think about your own connections to privilege and intersectionality with a series of in-class exercises. As self-identified black, lesbian, feminist, mother, poet, warrior, and cancer-survivor, Audre Lorde (1984) says, “There is no such thing as a single-issue struggle because we do not live single-issue lives.”

Theories of inequality - Power/Knowledge For those of you who have previously studied sociology, you were likely introduced to some of key thinkers in sociological theory: Marx, Weber, Durkheim, for example. In this course we reference these important thinkers, but we want to bring in a different theoretical perspective by thinking about the work of Michel Foucault and how his ideas of power/knowledge and discipline have shaped our thinking in new and exciting ways and given us new understandings of the dynamics of inequality. In the first half of this week’s workshop, we will apply Foucault’s thinking about power/knowledge, introduced in the learning module, and use a jigsaw reading exercise to help us work through some of these more complex ideas. In the second half of the workshop, we will undertake Assessment item 1(c), the third in a series of 4 in-class writing exercises. Today’s in-class assessment is worth 20% of your final mark. If you cannot make your workshop time, please see the Study Guide for options on how you can still complete this assessment item. Assessment 1(c) will focus on material from any of the previous weeks (Weeks 5-7).

Social inequality - Is change possible? Week 9 focuses on the evolution of inequality over time, examining individual fluctuations and life course perspectives. Associate Professor Paco Perales's recorded lecture poses the question: does being born poor mean staying poor? We will also delve into intergenerational transmission of advantage/disadvantage, exploring broader societal impacts. Transitioning from micro to macro analysis, we'll scrutinise the role of social institutions in shaping equal or unequal societies. Completing the online Learning Module before the seminar is essential for productive class discussions.

Responding to social Inequality – Solution focused social science So far we have spent the semester exploring social inequality – what it is, why it exists, how it can be measured, and its various often devastating impacts. For the last few weeks of semester we are looking at how we might respond, which links to the idea of “Solution focused social science”. Our learning module this week introduces this idea, suggesting that the role of social science is not simply to study phenomena, but to provide solutions towards resolving modern social challenges. As Wednesday of this week is a public holiday in Brisbane there are no workshops scheduled. However, we will be monitoring online communications should you have any queries about the online materials.

What strategies can we use for social transformation? Throughout this course we have highlighted the central concepts of agency and structure. We have spent the last section of the course looking at how structure operates. Now we flip to thinking about agency – first of all, how we exercise individual agency, as well as the limits of our individual agency. Then we begin thinking about collective agency, how we can work deliberately to create change in social structures. In the first half of this week’s class, we apply the ideas of individual and collective agency to the case study of fast fashion. How could we create change in this global human rights and environmental challenge? This will help you in your thinking about the final assessment piece for the course. In the second half of the workshop we will undertake Assessment item 1(d), the third in a series of 4 in-class writing exercises. Today’s in-class assessment is worth 20% of your final mark. If you cannot make your workshop time, please see the Study Guide for options on how you can still complete this assessment item. Assessment 1(d) will focus on material from any of the previous weeks (Weeks 8-10).

How do I plan for future impact? Join us for our final learning module, where we focus on employability and help you use what you have learned in this course, in your thinking about future career options. There is no formal workshop during week 12. You are encouraged to use this time prepare your final assessment item for the course (Assessment 2). Your tutors will be available for drop-in consultation. They will advise you of the arrangements for this.

Assessment 2 There is no formal workshop during week 13. You are encouraged to use this time prepare your final assessment item for the course (Assessment 2 - due Friday 2pm this week). Your tutors will be available for drop-in consultation. They will advise you of the arrangements for this.
